content.full_text:
February 23, 1994


Russell P. Rumansoff
Herron and Rumansoff
530 St. Johns Place
Hemet, CA 92543

Re:  Your Request for Informal Assistance
     Our File No. I-94-045

Dear Mr. Rumansoff:

     This is in response to your letter requesting advice regard-
ing your responsibilities as a Hemet Planning Commissioner under
the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the "Act").¹ Since your advice request does not refer to a
specific governmental decision but seeks general guidance, we are
treating your request as one for informal assistance.²

     Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place. In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us. The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC ops. 71.)

                         QUESTIONS

     1.  How much involvement may you have with city staff
regarding the processing of your client's projects?




¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento, CA  95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-94-045
Page 2


     2.  May an employee of your firm or your partner:

          (a) Contact staff with respect to your client's projects
in your name?

          (b) Represent your clients before the planning
commission? :

     3.  May you or your firm provide services to the city?

                              CONCLUSIONS

     1.  You may perform services in your private capacity with
respect to the clients of your firm. In addition, you may prepare
drawings and submissions of an architectural nature to be used by
a client in connection with a proceeding before any agency.

     However, you may not appear before your own agency nor
contact your agency, or any officer or employee of the agency,
other than necessary contact with agency staff concerning the
processing or evaluation of the drawings or submissions prepared
by the official.

     2(a) and (b).  Neither the conduct of your partner nor of
your employees is restricted by the Act so long as: (1) you
disqualify yourself from making, participating in the making, or
influencing the decisions and (2) your partner or employee is not
merely serving as an intermediary or vehicle for you to
communicate with your agency or otherwise influence the decision.
Thus, the partner or employee may not contact city staff or
present projects "in your name" nor may you be identified in
relation to any project, other than in the context of the
exception noted above.

     3.  The Act does not prohibit your firm from contracting with
the City of Hemet. However, other provisions of law may apply to
such a situation, such as Government Code Section 1090. You
should contact the Attorney General's Office with respect to
questions about Section 1090.

     The Act does prohibit your participation in decisions having
a material financial effect on your firm (such as the awarding of
such a contract). In addition, the individuals providing services
to the city may, under some circumstances, be considered public
officials under the Act.

                              FACTS

     You were recently appointed to the planning commission for
the City of Hemet.

Our File No. I-94-045
Page 3


     In your private capacity, you are a licensed architect and a
co-owner of Herron and Rumansoff, an architectural firm that does
business in Hemet. You stated that when you represent clients for
your firm, you normally prepare architectural drawings and
complete applications which include your name. In addition, even
when other members of your firm provide such services, you stated
that your firm name is identified.

                              ANALYSIS

     The Act was adopted by the voters in California by initiative
in 1974. The purpose of the conflict-of-interest provisions of
the Act was to ensure that public officials, whether elected or
appointed, would perform their duties in an impartial manner, free
from any bias caused by their own financial interests or the
financial interests of persons who have supported them. (Section
81001(b).)

     In furtherance of this goal, Section 87100 of the Act
prohibits any public official from making, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest.

     A "public official" is defined in Section 82048 and
Regulation 18700 as every natural person who is a member, officer,
employee, or consultant of a state or local government agency. As
a member of the Hemet Planning Commission, you are a "public
official" as defined in the Act. (Section 82048.)

     Section 87103 provides:

          An official has a financial interest in a
     decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it
     is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
     have a material financial effect, distinguishable
     from its effect on the public generally, on the
     official or a member of his or her immediate family
     or on:

          (a) Any business entity in which the
     public official has a direct or indirect
     investment worth one thousand dollars ($1,000)
     or more.

                              * * *

          (c) Any source of income, other than
     gifts and other than loans by a commercial
     lending institution in the regular course of
     business on terms available to the public
     without regard to official status, aggregating
     two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in
     value provided to, received by or promised to

Our File No. I-94-045
Page 4


the public official within 12 months prior to
the time when the decision is made.

(d) Any business entity in which the
public official is a director, officer,
partner, trustee, employee, or holds any
position of management.

Pursuant to Section 87103, your architectural firm and the
clients of your firm are economic interests. Thus, you may not
make, participate in making, or in any way attempt to use your
official position to influence a governmental decision which will
have a reasonably foreseeable³ material financial effect on the
firm or clients.

Appearing Before the Planning Commission

Clearly, the provisions of the Act would prohibit you from
voting on the projects of your client or your firm. (Regulation
18700.) In addition, the Act prohibits you from contacting any
member, officer, employee, or consultant of your agency or any
agency under the appointive or budgetary control of your agency:

With regard to a governmental decision which
is within or before an official's agency or an
agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary
control of his or her agency, the official is
attempting to use his or her official position to
influence the decision if, for the purpose of
influencing the decision, the official contacts, or
appears before, or otherwise attempts to influence,
any member, officer, employee or consultant of the
agency. Attempts to influence include, but are not
limited to, appearances or contacts by the official
on behalf of a business entity, client, or
customer.

Regulation 18700.1(a).

Pursuant to Regulation 18700.1, you may not appear before
your own agency on behalf of any client. In addition, Regulation
18700.1 would prohibit any contacts, appearances, or other
attempts to influence any member, officer, employee, or consultant
of your agency. Attempts to influence include, but are not

3 Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably
foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made depends on
the facts of each particular case. An effect is considered
reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that
it will occur. Certainty is not required. However, if an effect
is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable.
(Downey Cares v. Downey Community Development Com. (1987) 196
Cal.App.3d 983; In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

Our File No. I-94-045
Page 5


limited to, appearances or contacts by the official on behalf of a
business entity, client, or customer. Thus, the Act would
prohibit you from writing letters to the planning commission, and
any other communications with the planning commission that include
your name.

Exceptions

Regulation 18700.1(b) provides:

     [A]n official is not attempting to use his or
her official position to influence a governmental
decision ... if the official:

* * *

     (4) Prepares drawings or submissions of an
architectural, engineering or similar nature to be
used by a client in connection with a proceeding
before any agency. However, this provision applies
only if the official has no other direct oral or
written contact with the agency with regard to the
client's proceeding before the agency except for
necessary contact with agency staff concerning the
processing or evaluation of the drawings or
submissions prepared by the official.

                              (Emphasis added.)

The basis for the exception was set forth in the staff
memorandum submitted to the Commission in connection with the
exception. The memorandum stated:

     The cities were basically concerned about
limitations on the professional activities of
planning commissioners and city councilmembers who
are architects and engineers. There was general
agreement that planning commissioner/architects
should not actually represent clients before the
planning commission. It was also agreed that such
architects and engineers should be able to prepare
the drawings and plans for clients, even though the
drawings and the plans would have to be submitted
to the planning commission. There seemed to be
general consensus among the city attorneys that
this exception is necessary in order to allow the
local architects and engineers to continue to
practice their profession when they act as planning
commissioners or councilmembers

Consequently, you may prepare architectural drawings or
submissions to be used by a client in connection with a proceeding
before your own agency. Please note, however, that the

Our File No. I-94-045
Page 6


preparation of nontechnical documents or other materials would not
fall within this exception.⁴ (Levinger Advice Letter, No. I-88-
328.) In addition, you are still prohibited from appearing before
the planning commission.

Furthermore, while Regulation 18700.1 expressly prohibits
direct oral or written contact with the agency with regard to the
client's project, it does permit necessary contact with agency
staff concerning the processing or evaluation of the drawings or
submissions prepared by the official. The provision has been
narrowly construed by the Commission staff to permit the official
only to respond to questions from the staff of the agency
concerning the submissions or plans. (Holbert Advice Letter, No.
I-90-080; West Advice Letter, No. A-88-413.)

Other Employees

     The Act does not prohibit an employee of your architectural
business or your partner from representing an applicant before the
planning commission or the city council. (Byrne Advice Letter,
No. A-93-153.) This would be the case even where your firm's
letterhead, which includes your name, was used by the other
person. You are, however, prohibited from communicating with your
fellow commissioners and staff regarding the matter.⁵

Contracting with the City

     The Act does not prohibit a public official from contracting
with his or her own agency. However, other laws may prohibit such
a relationship. (See, Section 1090, et seq.) Please note that
Section 1090 is not a part of the Political Reform Act. Since the
Commission's jurisdiction is limited to the Political Reform Act,
the Commission has no jurisdiction to interpret or enforce Section
1090. Questions concerning Section 1090 should be addressed to
the Attorney General's Office.

     As discussed above, the Act would prohibit the official's
participation in the contract decision. In addition, under some
circumstances, an individual who is not an employee of a


_________________________
⁴ It might be advisable to separate out the tasks associated with
a client's project that will require communications with city
staff (other than technical drawings and submissions which fall
within the exception discussed above).

⁵ To the extent that the Levinger Advice Letter, supra, and the
Freeman Advice Letter (No. I-90-664) differ from these conclusion,
they are superseded.

Our File No. I-94-045
Page 7


governmental agency may qualify as a "consultant."6 Regulation
18700(a)(2) defines the term "consultant" as follows:

"Consultant" shall include any natural person
who provides, under contract, information, advice,
recommendation or counsel to a state or local
government agency, provided, however, that
"consultant" shall not include a person who:

(A) Conducts research and arrives at
conclusions with respect to his or her
rendition of information, advice,
recommendation or counsel independent of the
control and direction of the agency or of any
agency official, other than normal contract
monitoring; and

(B) Possesses no authority with respect
to any agency decision beyond the rendition of
information, advice, recommendation or
counsel.

Regulation 18700(a)(2).

Of course whether any employees of your firm would qualify as
consultants would depend on the specific facts surrounding the
contract, facts we do not have before us.

If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.7

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division


6 The definition of "consultant" in Regulation 18700 is currently
under consideration for amendment by the Commission. Thus, the
definition is subject to change.

7 Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.
